1: David flees to Jonathan, asking “What have I done? What is my wrong? What is my sin before your father?” — the declaration of innocence from the one pursued without cause. Christ before Pilate: “What evil has he done?” (Matthew 27:23); Jesus's own declaration “which of you convicts me of sin?” (John 8:46). The innocent anointed pursued by hostile authority with no legitimate charge — David to Jonathan, Jesus to the court — establishes the structural injustice that the NT resurrection reverses: the innocent one vindicated by YHWH after unjust condemnation.
2: Jonathan insists: “Far from it — you will not die! My father never acts without telling me.” Jonathan's confident defense of David is based on his belief in his own access to his father's counsel — he assumes he would know if David were truly condemned. The disciples' confidence before the Passion narrative has the same shape: “Lord, I am ready to go with you both to prison and to death” (Luke 22:33). In both cases the defender's confidence reflects a partial understanding of the situation — Jonathan doesn't yet know how determined Saul is; Peter doesn't yet know his own weakness.
3: David swears: “Your father knows that I have found favor with you.” David's insight into his own situation — that he is already effectively condemned — is more accurate than Jonathan's. Christ's foreknowledge of his own death is the NT counterpart: “The Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of men” (Mark 9:31; Luke 9:44). The anointed's clear-eyed acceptance of the hostility directed at him, against the well-meaning denial of his closest friend, is the pattern of the Passion prediction cycle.
4: Jonathan: “Whatever you want me to do, I will do it.” The total covenant availability of the covenant friend — offering unconditional service — is the posture Christ's disciples are called to embody. John 15:14-15: “You are my friends if you do what I command you.” Jonathan's “whatever you want me to do” is the model of covenant friendship in which the friend's will is aligned with the anointed's will. The disciples are called into this relationship — not slaves who do not know the master's purpose, but friends who share in it.
5: David proposes the new moon test: his empty seat will reveal Saul's true intentions. The indirect revelation of hostility through a deliberate absence is a narrative device: the empty seat exposes what is hidden in Saul's heart. Christ's parables similarly use indirect methods to reveal what is hidden — the parable of the tenants (Matthew 21:33-44) exposes the religious leaders' intentions in a form they recognize only after it is told (“they knew he was talking about them,” v45). The indirect method of truth-disclosure characterizes the wisdom of the anointed in a hostile environment.
6: The cover story — David went to Bethlehem for a family sacrifice — uses the legitimate religious calendar as protective cover. The observance of legitimate covenant life as the cover under which the anointed moves is a type of the incarnation's hiddenness: the eternal Son concealed in the ordinary rhythms of Jewish family life in Nazareth (Luke 2:51-52) — “hidden” from those who had no eyes to see him in the ordinary family from Galilee. Divine authority concealed within completely ordinary human form.
7: If Saul answers calmly, peace; if he flares up in anger, Jonathan will know Saul has decided to harm David. The diagnostic test that reveals the heart of the hostile party is the pattern Christ uses in multiple contexts: the question about David's son (Matthew 22:41-46), the question about tribute to Caesar (Matthew 22:17-21), the healing on the Sabbath performed openly while the Pharisees watch (Mark 3:1-6). The anointed uses situations that reveal what is hidden in his opponents' hearts — and uses the revelation to communicate with those who have eyes to see.
8: “Show your covenant loyalty (ḥeseḏ) to your servant — you yourself brought me into a covenant before YHWH.” The appeal to prior covenant as the basis for present faithfulness is the logic of Christ's intercessory prayer: “I have given them the words you gave me” (John 17:8); “they were yours; you gave them to me” (1 Samuel 17:6). The covenant already made grounds the request for continued faithfulness. David appeals to the covenant Jonathan initiated; believers appeal to the covenant Christ ratified — the basis for confidence in continued covenant protection.
9: Jonathan: “If I knew my father had decided to harm you, I would warn you.” Jonathan's insistence on complete disclosure to David — sharing everything relevant to David's survival — is the model of the friend who withholds nothing. John 15:15: “I have called you friends, because everything I have heard from my Father I have made known to you.” Jesus's complete disclosure of the Father's purposes to his disciples is the fulfillment of what Jonathan pledges to David: the anointed's closest companion withholds nothing that affects the other's wellbeing.
10: David asks: “Who will let me know if your father answers you harshly?” — the practical problem of intelligence and communication across distance and danger. The anointed's need for reliable communication from his covenant friend despite the hostile intermediary environment is the structure of the Spirit's role in the church: “when the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you into all truth... he will tell you what is to come” (John 16:13). The arrow-signal system Jonathan devises is a covenant communication network operating beneath the surface of a hostile environment.
11: “Come, let's go out to the field” — the movement from the potentially observed indoor space to the private open field for the covenant oath. Private sacred space for covenant communication between the anointed and his covenant partner is consistent throughout the biblical narrative: Sinai (Moses and YHWH alone), the Mount of Transfiguration (Jesus with three disciples, away from the crowd), Gethsemane (Jesus withdrawing from the disciples to pray). The covenant friend takes the anointed aside for the most intimate communication.
12: Jonathan's oath: “By YHWH, the God of Israel — when I sound out my father tomorrow... and the news is good for David, will I not then send to you and disclose it?” The sworn covenant communication network — Jonathan as reliable intermediary between David's situation and YHWH's protection — is the pattern of the Spirit as the reliable intermediary between the Father's purposes and the church. John 16:14-15: “He will glorify me, because he will take from what is mine and declare it to you. Everything the Father has is mine; that is why I said the Spirit will take from what is mine and declare it to you.”
13: Jonathan invokes YHWH's punishment on himself if he fails to warn David when Saul means harm. The self-imprecation oath — placing oneself under divine sanction to guarantee faithfulness — is the highest level of covenant commitment. Christ's covenant commitment to his people has no conditional element or self-imprecation because his faithfulness is absolute: “if we are faithless, he remains faithful, for he cannot deny himself” (2 Timothy 2:13). Jonathan's oath is the human approximation of the divine faithfulness that only Christ fully embodies.
14: “If I am still alive, show me the steadfast love (ḥeseḏ) of YHWH — do not let me die.” Jonathan's vulnerability — asking for the protection of YHWH's covenant love through David — is the acknowledgment that his own fate is bound up with David's victory. The church's asking Christ for the ḥeseḏ of the new covenant — “your steadfast love is before my eyes, and I walk in your faithfulness” (Psalms 26:3) — rests on the same logic: if the anointed is victorious, those in covenant with him share the victory. Jonathan's request anticipates Paul's “if God is for us, who can be against us?” (Romans 8:31).
15: “Never cut off your steadfast love from my house, even when YHWH has cut off all of David's enemies from the face of the earth.” The covenant made across the dimension of David's future victory is the structure of the new covenant: the benefits of Christ's finished work applied retroactively and prospectively — to those before and after his coming who are in covenant with him. Jonathan secures covenant protection for his descendants from the future victorious king. Christ's new covenant blood secures eternal redemption for those in covenant with him, reaching backward to Abraham (Galatians 3:14) and forward to the consummation.
16: Jonathan makes a covenant with the house of David, invoking YHWH's judgment on David's enemies. The covenant between Jonathan's house and David's house — solemnized by the mutual enemy-imprecation — is the bilateral covenant structure. The new covenant is the supreme covenant in which YHWH himself is both parties (Gen 15: YHWH alone passes between the pieces) — making it unilaterally secure in a way no bilateral covenant can be. Jonathan's covenant with David requires David's faithfulness; Christ's covenant with his people is grounded in Christ's own faithfulness on both sides.
17: Jonathan made David swear again because of his love for David, for he loved him as his own soul (kĕnapšô). This is the covenant love that makes Jonathan's commitment absolute: he loves David as himself. This is the Christological love: “having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end” (John 13:1); “Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends” (John 15:13). Jonathan's love for David as his own soul is the human approximation of Christ's love for his covenant people — the love that gives everything including life itself.
18: “Tomorrow is the new moon feast, and your absence will be noticed — your seat will be empty.” The empty seat at the covenant feast reveals the anointed's absence and forces the hostile party's hand. The Lord's table in the new covenant is the feast at which the anointed's presence is proclaimed until he comes: “whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes” (1 Corinthians 11:26). The present feast is both absence (awaiting his return) and presence (he is with us wherever two or three gather in his name, Matthew 18:20).
19: “On the third day go quickly to the place where you hid when this trouble started, and stay beside the stone Ezel.” The third day as the appointed time for the decisive revelation of the anointed's situation — whether he is safe or in mortal danger — resonates with the resurrection pattern. “On the third day he will rise again” (Matthew 20:19) is the NT application of the third-day resurrection structure that runs through the OT (Hosea 6:2: “after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up”; Genesis 22:4; Jonah 1:17). The third day as the day of decisive divine action is embedded in both David's story and the resurrection.
20: “I will shoot three arrows to the side of it, as if I were shooting at a target.” The arrow-shooting as coded communication system — a message that appears to be practice but carries life-or-death information — is the structure of parabolic communication. Jesus speaks in parables so that “seeing they do not see, and hearing they do not hear, nor do they understand” (Matthew 13:13) — the message's meaning is veiled for the hostile audience while transparent to those with “ears to hear.” Jonathan's arrow-system communicates safely across hostile territory; Christ's parabolic speech communicates across the hostile territory of hardened hearts.
21: “If I call to him, ‘The arrows are on this side of you, pick them up’ — then come, for YHWH says it is safe for you.” The coded “come” versus “go” signal embedded in the arrow-retrieval instruction is covenant intelligence delivered through a seemingly innocent activity. The angels' “come” to the women at the empty tomb (“he has risen from the dead; he is going ahead of you into Galilee; there you will see him”, Matthew 28:7) is the post-resurrection “it is safe to come out” signal — the threat is past, the anointed is free, the disciples can emerge from their locked room.
22: “If I say to the boy, ‘The arrows are farther out’ — then go, for YHWH has sent you away.” The divine authorization embedded in the “go” signal — “YHWH has sent you away” — frames even the tactical escape as covenant commission. Christ's post-resurrection commissioning uses the same frame: “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Therefore go” (Matthew 28:18-19). The “go” that seems like flight is actually divine sending; the apparent retreat is covenant advance. David going from Saul's court into the wilderness is YHWH sending him to his next stage of preparation.
23: “As for everything we have agreed on between us — YHWH is witness between you and me forever.” The covenant ratified with YHWH as eternal witness is the highest possible covenant guarantee. The new covenant sealed with Christ's blood (Luke 22:20: “this cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood”) has YHWH not merely as witness but as the covenant-maker who guarantees his own faithfulness. “Forever” (ʿaḏ ʿôlām) in Jonathan's covenant becomes “eternal” (aiōnion) in the new covenant: “the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus... by the blood of the eternal covenant” (Hebrews 13:20).
24: David hides in the field; the new moon comes; the king sits to eat. The feast proceeds without the anointed — the covenant community's gathering in the absence of the one around whom the feast should be organized is the structure of the pre-Parousia church. Paul to the Corinthians: “you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes” (1 Corinthians 11:26) — the supper is eaten in the anointed's absence, pointing toward his return. Saul's feast without David is the distorted version; the church's table fellowship in Christ's name is the redeemed version of the same structure.
25: The seating arrangement around the table — Saul against the wall, Jonathan opposite, Abner at Saul's side, David's place empty — is a narrative snapshot of the court from which the anointed has been excluded. The Last Supper's table arrangement — Jesus in the center, the twelve around him, the betrayer physically present but spiritually absent — is the inverse: the anointed at the center of his covenant community, with the one who will hand him over occupying the empty-seat role.
26: Saul says nothing on the first day, assuming David is ceremonially unclean. The charitable interpretation extended to the absent one reflects the new moon feast's religious framework — absence might have a legitimate ritual explanation. The grace of interpreting absence charitably before moving to hostile conclusions is the structure of Christian love: “love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things” (1 Corinthians 13:7). Saul's temporary charity will expire by the second day; covenant love's charity is permanent.
27: On the second day Saul asks Jonathan: “Why hasn't Jesse's son come to the meal either yesterday or today?” The demotion from “David” to “Jesse's son” marks the shift from pretended neutrality to open contempt. The social derogation of the anointed — stripping his name and replacing it with a dismissive identification — is the linguistic equivalent of Pilate's placard: “Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews” (John 19:19) — intended as mockery, it accidentally proclaims the truth. The enemies' contemptuous labels for the anointed often contain more theological accuracy than they intend.
28: Jonathan relays David's cover story: David asked permission to attend his family's sacrifice at Bethlehem. Jonathan acts as intermediary, carrying David's message to the hostile king — the covenant friend as spokesperson for the absent anointed. The Holy Spirit as the Paraclete performs this mediating function for the absent Christ: “he will testify about me” (John 15:26); “he will take what is mine and declare it to you” (John 16:14). Jonathan speaks for David before the court that condemned him; the Spirit speaks for Christ before the world that rejected him.
29: “Our family has a sacrifice in the city and my brother insisted I be there.” The family's call preceding the king's summons — a legitimate religious obligation given priority over royal attendance — is the pattern of Acts 5:29: “we must obey God rather than men.” The disciples, like David, claim a higher obligation that supersedes the hostile authority's demands. The cover story David uses contains a genuine theological priority even if the specific circumstances are fabricated: covenant family obligations genuinely do take precedence over hostile political demands.
30: Saul erupts in fury at Jonathan, calling him son of a “perverse and rebellious woman.” The abuse directed at the advocate for the anointed — when direct attack on the anointed himself is not possible — is the consistent pattern. When the crowd cannot reach Jesus directly, they abuse his disciples (John 15:18-21: “if they persecuted me, they will also persecute you”). The hostility generated by the anointed's presence overflows onto his closest covenant partner when the anointed is out of reach.
31: “For as long as Jesse's son lives on this earth, neither you nor your kingdom will stand.” Saul's recognition that David's survival means the end of his own dynasty is an accurate theological reading of the situation — Saul understands the zero-sum nature of the kingship claim. But his zero-sum logic is wrong: David's reign does not destroy Jonathan but makes his descendants blessed through the covenant (2 Sam 9: Mephibosheth at David's table). Christ's kingdom is similarly understood as a zero-sum threat by those who oppose it — “if we let him go on like this, everyone will believe in him” (John 11:48) — but his reign exalts rather than eliminates those who receive him.
32: Jonathan challenges his father: “Why should he die? What has he done?” — the advocate's direct defense before the hostile judge. Pilate's “I find no fault in him” (John 18:38; 1 Samuel 19:4, 6) is the secular authority echoing the same defense. Jonathan's question (what crime justifies death?) and Pilate's declaration (no crime found) both establish the structural injustice of the proceedings against the anointed. Both advocates ultimately fail to prevent the condemnation — Jonathan is silenced by Saul's spear; Pilate is silenced by the crowd's demand.
33: Saul hurls his spear at Jonathan. The father's violence against his own son for defending the anointed is the pattern of apostolic suffering: “A person's enemies will be members of his own household” (Matthew 10:36, quoting Micah 7:6); “Brother will betray brother to death, and a father his child” (Matthew 10:21). The defense of the anointed ruptures family bonds and brings violence from the closest quarters. Jonathan now knows by personal experience what David told him: Saul is fully determined to eliminate the anointed.
34: Jonathan rises from the table furious and eats nothing, grieving for David and for his own humiliation. The grief of the covenant friend who has been personally struck for defending the anointed is the pattern of apostolic suffering. The disciples “left with great sorrow” (Matthew 26:22) when Jesus announced one of them would betray him. Jonathan's table-departure in grief mirrors the disciples' departure from the Last Supper into the night of Gethsemane — the feast that should have been joy becomes the occasion of grief because of the hostile powers that surround the anointed.
35: Jonathan goes out to the field at the appointed time with the young servant. The covenant communication system is now in operation — the private signal that will tell David whether to stay or flee. The Spirit's timing in directing the apostolic mission follows the same “appointed time” structure: Acts 16:6-10 (the Spirit prevents Paul from going to Asia and Bithynia, then directs him to Macedonia through the vision) — the covenant communication network operates according to divine timing, using indirect means to direct the anointed's mission across hostile territory.
36: Jonathan shoots the arrow that flies beyond the boy. The arrow going “farther out” carries the message: go, YHWH has sent you away. The “go” signal delivered through an apparently ordinary archery practice is the pattern of divine commissioning hidden in ordinary events. Christ's post-resurrection commissioning is similarly delivered through ordinary meals (the Emmaus road), a beach breakfast (John 21), and an ordinary hillside gathering (Matthew 28:16) — the great commission embedded in scenes that look unremarkable from the outside.
37: Jonathan calls out: “The arrow is farther out!” — the coded signal that carries the covenant intelligence. Hidden speech that carries covenant meaning in a hostile environment is the pattern of Christ's teaching in parables: the meaning accessible to disciples, opaque to opponents. “To you it has been given to know the secrets of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it has not been given” (Matthew 13:11). The arrow's message — “go” — is the same message Christ communicates to his disciples through the Spirit after the resurrection: “go into all the world” (Mark 16:15).
38: Jonathan calls “Hurry! Don't stop!” The urgency of the covenant commissioning — once the signal is given, swift action is required — mirrors the urgency of the apostolic mission. “I must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming when no one can work” (John 9:4). The anointed's mission has a timeline; the covenant friend's urgent “don't stop” is the Spirit's “the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few” (Matthew 9:37) — urgency proportionate to the stakes.
39: The boy knew nothing of the meaning; only Jonathan and David understood the signal. The private covenant language accessible only to those who share the covenant — opaque to the uninitiated observer — is the structure of the Spirit's illumination of Scripture and of Christ's teaching. “The person without the Spirit does not accept the things that come from the Spirit of God... but the person with the Spirit can evaluate all things” (1 Corinthians 2:14-15). The servant is present but comprehends nothing; Jonathan and David share the same knowledge because they share the same covenant.
40: Jonathan hands his weapons to the servant and sends him back to the city. The weapons — the instruments of the signal — are passed on to be carried into ordinary life, while the covenant encounter between Jonathan and David happens privately. The instruments of covenant communication (Scripture, the sacraments) are available to all; the covenant meeting they enable is personal and direct. The boy carries the weapons without knowing their significance; the church carries the forms of covenant communication whose full significance only the Spirit illuminates.
41: As soon as the boy is gone, David comes out from beside the mound, falls on his face and bows three times; they weep together, David most of all. The covenant farewell with mutual weeping — David most of all — is the intensity of covenant love at the moment of separation. The disciples' grief at the Last Supper (John 16:6: “because I have said these things to you, sorrow has filled your heart”) mirrors this — the covenant community weeping at the prospect of separation from the anointed. The tears are proportionate to the love; David weeping most is the anointed's grief at separation from his covenant partner.
42: “Go in peace — we have both sworn in YHWH's name: YHWH shall be between you and me, and between your offspring and my offspring, forever.” Jonathan's farewell is a benediction that extends the covenant across generations into eternity. The “go in peace” (lēḵ lĕšālôm) is the priestly blessing released over the departing one. Christ's farewell to his disciples: “Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you” (John 14:27); “As the Father has sent me, I am sending you” (John 20:21). The covenant farewell of both Jonathan and Christ releases the covenant partner into mission with the peace of the covenant attending them.
